transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Good evening everyone. Sprint 3 retrospective. Client signed off on the auth module Wednesday, so we're closing the sprint properly. I sent the retro agenda this morning — what went well, what didn't, and action items for Sprint 4. Let's go through it honestly. Rakesh, start us off with what went well.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: I'll start with the obvious win — auth module delivered on time. OIDC integration, token refresh logic, session management — all done, all signed off. And the test coverage Ajay's team achieved was genuinely impressive. We targeted 85%, delivered 91%. That's not just a metric — those tests caught 3 regressions during development that would have burned QA time otherwise.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I want to add something to the quality point. The daily code review process we started this sprint made a real difference. Issues got caught at the code level before they became QA findings. In previous sprints I'd raise a bug and it would sit for 2 days. This sprint, critical bugs were being patched same day most of the time. That's a cultural shift worth keeping.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Suraj spotted a token storage anti-pattern in my code on Day 3 — that could have been a security issue if it reached prod. I caught a redundant re-render in her auth state component. These cross-reviews took 20 minutes a day but saved hours of rework. That should absolutely continue in Sprint 4.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: Agreed. And from my side — the scope was mostly stable this sprint. After Day 3 the backlog didn't change, which meant I could plan my week. Sprint 2 had scope changes all the way through to the last day. This sprint was much better in that regard.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Thank you. I deliberately held the line on scope after Day 3. Good to hear it made a difference. Okay — what didn't go well. I want the real version, not the diplomatic one.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: Staging environment parity. This is the second sprint in a row I'm saying this exact thing in a retro. We had three separate incidents this sprint where staging configuration didn't match what production expected. That caused false test failures and rework. One time I marked a test as failed when the code was actually fine — it was just a staging config difference. Combined, those incidents cost us roughly two days of testing time.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: And the root cause is always the same. Central infra makes changes to prod configuration without notifying our team. I'm not in their change management loop. By the time I find out — usually when Ajay hits a failure — the damage is done. I've raised this in three separate infra sync meetings. It has not been actioned. I need to be cc'd on prod infrastructure change notifications. That's a simple fix that nobody has implemented.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Vivek — what specifically do you need? Give me the ask in one or two sentences so I can action it.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Two things. First — I need to be added to the distribution list for all prod infra change notifications. Email or Teams, doesn't matter. Second — I want to build a daily automated config comparison script that checks key values between staging and prod and alerts if they drift. I catch it before Ajay does, not after.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: The config drift script is a good idea. Probably 2-3 hours of work. And there's a related issue I've been meaning to raise. Right now central infra maintains prod and staging as completely separate systems. They don't guarantee parity. For Sprint 4 we're going to be doing a production payment API go-live. If prod and staging have different certificate authorities, different network security group rules, different environment variables — we could be testing perfectly on staging and then hit completely unexpected failures in prod. That's not a hypothetical. That is a real risk for Sprint 4.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: I've been saying this. Staging has a self-signed SSL cert. I flagged this last week in the infra sync. If prod has a proper CA-signed cert — which it should — our integration tests on staging tell us almost nothing about how the payment calls will behave in prod. I'm going to raise this again next week before Sprint 4 starts. But unless someone from this side follows up with infra leadership, it goes nowhere.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Vivek, put that SSL cert concern in writing — email to me and Vinod sir before Monday. We'll escalate it formally before Sprint 4 starts. That's a commitment.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: I'll send it tonight.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: Another thing that didn't go well — the client feedback loop was slow. Three times this sprint I built something, showed it in the demo, and the client came back 5 or 6 days later asking for changes. By then I'd moved on to something else and context-switching back cost time. If we could get client feedback within 48 hours I'd save probably half a day per cycle.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: That's fair and that's on me. The client's point of contact was often unavailable. I should have escalated earlier to get a backup reviewer assigned. I'll set up a formal review cadence with the client before Sprint 4 starts — 48-hour response SLA, and a named backup contact for when the primary is unavailable.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: I want to raise something about infra dependencies more broadly. In Sprint 3 we raised our infra requests on Day 2 of the sprint. Then we waited 2-3 days for responses. That pattern cost us time. For Sprint 4 — we're going into a payment API go-live with a Wednesday deadline. If we have any infra requests, they need to be raised before the sprint starts, not after it starts.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Exactly. And to do that we need Vivek in the Sprint 4 planning session. He's the one who knows what infra is needed. If he's not in planning, we discover the dependencies in standups and then we're already behind.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: I've been asking for this for two sprints. Please include me in planning. I promise I won't make it longer — I just need to know what's coming so I can flag things early.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Vivek is in Sprint 4 planning, no question. Rupali, that's confirmed. When's the planning session?

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Tuesday May 5, 11am. I'll send the invite tonight Vivek.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: One last thing from me. I'd like a basic smoke test suite that runs automatically every morning at 8:30am and tells me whether the environment is healthy before standup. Right now I find environment problems when I run my first real test case, which wastes my setup time. Five to ten checks — can the DB be reached, is the service running, can it respond to a basic API call. That's all I need.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: I can set that up. Before Sprint 4 Day 1. Basic health check, results posted to the team channel automatically.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Good. Let's capture all the action items clearly. Vivek: SSL cert concern in writing tonight, cc drift script before Sprint 4, smoke tests before Sprint 4 Day 1, added to prod infra change notifications. Rupali: client feedback cadence sorted before Sprint 4, Vivek included in planning Tuesday. Everyone: daily code reviews continue as a sprint ritual.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Good retro. I want to see the action items tracked in JIRA before Monday — not sitting in this call recording. Rupali, that's on you. Sprint 4 has a hard go-live deadline. The infra issues we've been tolerating for two sprints cannot repeat when we're doing a production payment deployment. Let's make sure we actually close these action items this time.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: JIRA tickets tonight. And I'll follow up with Vivek personally on Monday morning to make sure the infra requests are in motion. Have a good weekend everyone. Sprint 4 — let's start it right.
